text: The CT scan reveals a five-centimeter lipoma located within the sigmoid colon.
The surgeon has completed the process of freeing up the anatomical structures, likely involving the sigmoid colon and rectum.
The surgeon observes a floppy, redundant sigmoid colon, indicating its excessive length and mobility.
The lipoma is visible within the colon, occupying most of the lumen and appearing to be of considerable size.
The surgeon navigates through the thin, floppy mesentery of the sigmoid colon, using a vessel sealer to advance towards the proximal resection margin, facilitating a straightforward resection.
The surgeon finishes the dissection at the rectosigmoid junction, preparing to use the vessel sealer for further tissue manipulation.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis, removing burnt tissue from the surgical site.
The surgeon opens the proximal colon and carefully cleans off the surrounding fat to prepare for securing a purse string suture.
The surgeon prepares to extract the specimen transanally, with the right lower quadrant port undocked for potential assistance, although it was ultimately not required.
The surgeon uses a thoracic ring forcep to gently extract the specimen, with a visible lipoma extruding during the process. The specimen is removed without excessive force on the rectum.
The surgeon uses two points of retraction to stabilize the anvil, then pulls the edges of the colon over it, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon applies a purse string suture around the anvil, aiming to create a secure cuff of tissue without excess. The suture technique is adjusted to achieve optimal tension and tissue management.
The surgeon prepares the anvil and begins placing a purse string suture around the rectum, ensuring the suture encircles the tissue adequately for secure closure.
The surgeon maneuvers around the right side of the rectum, extending the suture beyond the initial starting point to create overlap, which aids in cinching the tissue tightly.
The surgeon cinches the purse string suture tightly around the rectum, preparing to deploy a 33mm EEA stapler. The stapler's spike is aligned to pass through the center of the purse string.
The surgeon approaches from below and uses the console to align the two components, then returns between the legs to fire the anastomosis.
The surgeon performs a leak test using a rigid proctoscope, ensuring the scope reaches up to and above the anastomosis site.
